name: 100% Automation Threshold
one_liner: Don't ship semi-automation: push past 95% accuracy to full reliability.
definition: The principle that an automated task is only valuable if it works 100% of the time; 95% accuracy means a human must still monitor, defeating the purpose.
explanation: Cat Wu argues that users often stop at 90-95% accuracy, but that last 5-10% is essential. She suggests investing the effort to teach the model preferences and give feedback until it's fully reliable, because a partially working automation still requires human oversight.
when_to_use: When automating repetitive tasks that you want to fully delegate
When the cost of errors is high or you want to trust the automation completely
metric_or_method: Task success rate measured on a representative test set
source_excerpt: If an automation doesn't work 100% of the time, it's not really an automation, and that last five to 10% does take more time.
codex_prompt_template: Write an eval that checks if an automated task achieves 100% success rate on a representative test set. The task is: [TASK_DESCRIPTION]. Specify failure modes and require zero human intervention across all test cases.
when_not_to_use: When automation is a hobby or learning exercise without production requirement
If the task has extremely low cost of failure and 95% is acceptable
common_pitfalls: Settling for 'good enough' and still having to check work
Underestimating effort to reach the last 5-10%
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)

name: A/B Experimentation with Control Group for AEO
one_liner: Isolate AEO impact by splitting questions into test and control groups.
definition: A method to evaluate the effectiveness of AEO strategies by comparing a test group (where you apply a tactic) against a control group (where you do nothing), tracking changes in answer appearance and rank over time.
explanation: Due to natural variance in LLM answers and increasing LLM usage, a control group is essential. Take a set of questions, split them into test and control, apply the tactic only to the test group, monitor both for a few weeks before/after, and compare. Repeat the experiment to ensure reproducibility.
when_to_use: When you want to measure the true impact of an AEO tactic (e.g., creating a YouTube video, commenting on Reddit, paying for affiliate mentions)
When you want to avoid false positives from unrelated changes or seasonality
metric_or_method: Change in share of voice or average rank between test and control groups over time.
source_excerpt: So what I would do is I would take 100 different questions, half of them I will intervene, half of them I won't. Or let's say, let's take 200 questions. So 100 of the questions, I'm not going to do anything, so that's my control group. [...] Test group would be, 'I'm now going to comment on Reddit threads', so let's test that. [...] And then the stuff that went up when the control group did not worked, and the stuff that didn't did not, and then reproduce it.
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script to simulate an A/B experiment for AEO. Given a list of [QUESTIONS], split into test and control groups. For test group, apply [TACTIC] (e.g., create YouTube video). Generate synthetic rank data for both groups over [WEEKS] weeks. Compute average rank change and share of voice difference. Output a summary table.
when_not_to_use: When you cannot feasibly isolate a test and control group
When the expected effect is extremely large and obvious
common_pitfalls: Natural variance in answers can obscure results without a control group
Failing to reproduce results – one study may be coincidental
Not accounting for increasing LLM traffic over time
source_title: The ultimate guide to AEO: How to get ChatGPT to recommend your product | Ethan Smith (Graphite)

name: Acceptance rate
one_liner: Track what users actually keep: acceptance rate measures the proportion of AI suggestions users commit.
definition: A metric that measures the proportion of AI-generated suggestions (e.g., code completions, responses) that users accept and commit. It indicates the consistency and helpfulness of the AI.
explanation: Ryan J. Salva from GitHub notes that Copilot's acceptance rate is ~35%, meaning developers commit 35% of suggestions. The 'good enough' rate varies by use case. The best way to find the right threshold is to ask customers if the feature makes their job easier. Acceptance rate is a direct feedback loop on model quality and user satisfaction.
when_to_use: When evaluating AI features that generate suggestions (e.g., code completion, chatbot replies)
When you need a quantitative measure of AI helpfulness
When iterating on prompt or model improvements
metric_or_method: Percentage of AI suggestions accepted by users
source_excerpt: "GitHub Copilot has an acceptance rate of 35%. That is to say, developers commit 35% of Copilot suggestions into their code editor. A ‘good enough’ acceptance rate will differ based on the use case and customer cohort. The best way to find out what’s right for you is to ask your customers, ‘Is this making your job easier?’"
codex_prompt_template: Generate a Python script that calculates the acceptance rate for an AI feature (e.g., code completions, chatbot replies). The script should read a CSV file with columns: suggestion_id, user_id, timestamp, accepted (0 or 1). Output the overall acceptance rate and optionally breakdown by user segment. Use [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION] to describe the feature. Save results to a JSON file.
when_not_to_use: When user acceptance is not a meaningful action (e.g., passive consumption of AI output)
When the AI output is rarely rejected explicitly (e.g., automatic summaries that users may not see)
common_pitfalls: Acceptance rate can be inflated if users feel obligated to accept (e.g., in a forced workflow)
Not accounting for context: some suggestions are easier to accept than others
Comparing rates across different use cases without adjusting for difficulty
source_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products

name: AI as a tutor / personalized learning eval
one_liner: Turn ChatGPT into a personal tutor that quizzes, explains, and adapts to each student's style.
definition: Using AI models like ChatGPT as a personalized tutor that can adapt to a student's learning style, quiz them, and explain concepts in different ways.
explanation: Bret Taylor describes using ChatGPT to tutor his kids: quizzing before tests, explaining Shakespeare passages, and adapting to visual or reading preferences. This is both an evaluation tool (quizzing) and a teaching tool.
when_to_use: When providing personalized education at scale
When students need immediate feedback
When teachers are overburdened
metric_or_method: Student performance improvement
source_excerpt: My kids use ChatGPT to quiz them before a test. You can use audio mode or chat mode. It's better than cue cards. My daughter took home a Shakespeare book, she took a picture of a page she didn't understand and ChatGPT explained it to her way better than I would've as well.
codex_prompt_template: Create a Python script that uses an AI model (like GPT-4) to act as a personalized tutor for a student studying [SUBJECT]. The tutor should: 1) Ask the student questions from a predefined quiz bank to test their knowledge, 2) Provide hints and explanations for wrong answers, adapting to the student's learning style (e.g., visual, reading, audio), 3) Track performance over time and adjust difficulty. Use a simple command-line or GUI interface.
when_not_to_use: When high-stakes evaluation requires human proctoring
When the model may hallucinate incorrect information
common_pitfalls: Over-reliance on AI without human guidance
AI may give incorrect answers on specialized topics
source_title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more

name: AI-assisted Code Review
one_liner: Let AI review AI-generated code, then humans do acceptance testing instead of line-by-line review.
definition: A review process where AI-generated code is reviewed by another AI model, and humans perform acceptance testing rather than detailed line-by-line review.
explanation: The Claude Code team uses this approach: they use one Claude to generate code and another Claude to review it. Humans then do acceptance testing instead of traditional line-by-line code review, recognizing that the AI is generally right and pull requests are too large for humans to review manually.
when_to_use: When AI generates large volumes of code
When team is confident in AI code quality
When pull request size exceeds human review capacity
metric_or_method: Acceptance testing by humans + AI review pass rate
source_excerpt: They've just realized Claude is generally right and it's producing pull requests that are probably larger than most people are going to be able to review. So can you use a different Claude to review it and then do the human almost acceptance testing more than trying to review line by line.
codex_prompt_template: You are an AI code reviewer. Review the following AI-generated code for [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Identify any bugs, security issues, or logical errors. Provide a pass/fail verdict and a brief explanation. Code: [CODE_SNIPPET]
when_not_to_use: When code is safety-critical or high-risk
When AI accuracy is not yet reliable
When team lacks trust in the AI's output
common_pitfalls: Loss of code maintainability
Reduced human understanding of codebase
Potential for cascading AI errors unnoticed
source_title: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)

name: AI Detector Accuracy Evaluation
one_liner: Validate AI detectors by comparing false positive rates on generated vs. real articles.
definition: A methodology to evaluate the accuracy of an AI detector by generating thousands of AI-generated articles and comparing against real articles from Common Crawl, measuring false positive rate.
explanation: Ethan Smith describes testing Surfer SEO's AI detector by generating thousands of AI articles and comparing them to real articles from Common Crawl (pre-ChatGPT). They measured an 8% false positive rate, indicating high accuracy.
when_to_use: When you need to validate the reliability of an AI detection tool before using it for analysis.
metric_or_method: False positive rate
source_excerpt: So we used Surfer SEO's AI detector. [...] So we did that by generating thousands of AI-generated articles and it was very predictive. And then we looked at real articles, we did that two different ways. [...] And then the false positive rate was around 8%, so basically the AI detector is very accurate.
codex_prompt_template: Generate [NUM_SAMPLES] AI-written articles on [TOPIC] and [NUM_SAMPLES] real articles from [SOURCE] (e.g., Common Crawl). Use [AI_DETECTOR_NAME] to classify each, then compute false positive rate (real articles flagged as AI) and false negative rate (AI articles missed). Output summary statistics and a confusion matrix. [PLACEHOLDER]
when_not_to_use: When you lack access to a large corpus of pre-ChatGPT human-written content or cannot generate many AI articles.
common_pitfalls: Assuming AI detectors are accurate without validation; using post-ChatGPT human content as baseline.
source_title: The ultimate guide to AEO: How to get ChatGPT to recommend your product | Ethan Smith (Graphite)

name: AI supervising AI
one_liner: Catch AI mistakes with a second AI reviewer, boosting accuracy to 99%.
definition: A technique where an AI agent that produces code (or other output) is monitored by another AI agent that reviews the output for errors. If the first agent is 90% accurate, the reviewer can catch 90% of the remaining errors, leading to 99% accuracy.
explanation: This pattern leverages multiple AI models in a pipeline. The first model generates output (e.g., code), and the second model reviews that output for errors. Since reviewing is often easier than generating from scratch, the reviewer can be highly effective at catching mistakes, compounding accuracy.
when_to_use: When you need high reliability from AI-generated outputs
When you have access to multiple AI models with complementary strengths
When the cost of errors is high (e.g., production code, customer-facing agents)
When generating code at scale
When human oversight is the bottleneck
When you want to catch errors automatically
metric_or_method: Accuracy rate after review; error detection rate of reviewer
source_excerpt: I think this idea of self-reflection in agents is really important. Having AI supervise the AI is actually very effective. Just think about it this way, if you produce an AI agent that's right 90% of the time, that's not that great, but how hard would it be to make another AI agent to find the errors the other 10% of the time? That might be a tractable problem. And if that thing's right 90% of the time, just for argument's sake, you can wire those things together and have something that's right 99% of the time.
codex_prompt_template: Design an AI supervision pipeline for [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]: use one model to generate outputs and a second model to review them for errors, then output the final accuracy improvement.
when_not_to_use: When latency is critical (two-stage processing adds time)
When the cost of running a second model is prohibitive
When the task is simple and a single model already achieves sufficient accuracy
When the AI model is not reliable enough for self-review
When you need a human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions
common_pitfalls: Assuming the supervisor model is always right – it also has a failure rate
Overlooking that reviewing is not always easier than generating; sometimes it's equally hard
Not accounting for the added cost and latency
AI may hallucinate or miss its own errors
Over-relying on self-reflection without external validation
source_title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more

name: Answer Engine Optimization (AEO)
one_liner: Track your brand's share of voice across LLM answer engines by sampling multiple question variations.
definition: A metric that captures how frequently and prominently a brand or product is cited across LLM answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, etc.), accounting for random sampling and question variations.
explanation: Since LLM answers change stochastically, you need to ask the same question multiple times and also ask variants. Track your average rank and frequency of appearance across different surfaces (e.g., ChatGPT, Perplexity). This is an evolution of keyword tracking for the AEO era.
when_to_use: When evaluating your brand's presence in LLM-generated answers
When comparing against competitors
When measuring the impact of AEO tactics over time
When you want to be recommended by an LLM for a product or service
For early-stage startups without high domain authority
When your own site lacks authority
To quickly start appearing in LLM answers
For brands that can get authentic mentions on community sites
metric_or_method: Share of voice = (number of times your brand appears) / (total number of answer runs) across surfaces and question variations.
source_excerpt: So how often am I showing up? What's my average rank? And that's answer tracking. [...] You need to ask the question multiple times, and you need to ask the variance of the question to truly know how frequently you're showing up.
codex_prompt_template: Write a script that simulates sampling brand appearances in LLM answers. For a given brand [BRAND_NAME] and a set of questions [QUESTION_SET], generate random answers from different LLMs (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini) 10 times each. Count how many times the brand appears and compute the share of voice as (appearances / total runs). Output the share of voice for each LLM and the combined average.
when_not_to_use: When you only care about a single LLM and a single query
When you have no way to automate or collect tracking data
When you only care about direct link clicks
For very niche products where LLM queries are rare
When you cannot produce authentic content on third-party platforms
When your brand has low share of voice
When you have no existing customer interactions
For very generic broad queries
common_pitfalls: Not asking multiple times – single answer is unreliable
Ignoring question variants
Only tracking one surface (e.g., ChatGPT) and missing others
Confusing share of voice with referral traffic
Assuming SEO is the same as AEO
Focusing only on ranking a single link
Using spammy tactics like fake accounts
Spamming Reddit with fake accounts

name: Attitudinal segmentation for evaluation
one_liner: Don't average away AI skeptics and embracers—segment by attitude to avoid tepid tea.
definition: A user testing and evaluation approach that segments users based on their attitudes toward AI (AI embracers vs. AI skeptics) rather than traditional functional needs, to avoid averaging out engagement and creating a product that satisfies no one.
explanation: When evaluating AI products, it's crucial to recognize that attitudes toward AI drive engagement patterns more than functional needs. Hilary Gridley advocates for longitudinal validation (testing long enough to see beyond novelty), high-touch testing (e.g., Slack groups instead of surveys), and careful attitudinal segmentation to avoid 'tepid tea'—a product that satisfies no one.
when_to_use: When evaluating product-market fit for AI products
When user engagement shows a steep churn cliff after novelty wears off
When you suspect that AI skeptics are rejecting your product despite meeting their functional needs
metric_or_method: Longitudinal validation of engagement with attitudinal segmentation
source_excerpt: "As a result, I’ve had to rethink our user testing approach from the ground up. When testing AI products, I focus on the following: 1. Longitudinal validation: Are we testing for a long enough period to understand how engagement changes once the novelty wears off? 2. High-touch testing: Are we staying close enough to users to understand how their attitudes, which drive engagement patterns, change daily? We’re experimenting with user Slack groups instead of traditional surveys and one-on-one qualitative interviews. 3. Attitudinal segmentation: Are we including both AI embracers and AI skeptics in our early testing groups? Crucially, are we segmenting them carefully to avoid averaging out their engagement and creating ‘tepid tea’—a product that satisfies no one?"
codex_prompt_template: Given user engagement data for [AI_FEATURE_DESCRIPTION], write a Python script that segments users into AI embracers and AI skeptics based on survey responses about comfort with AI. For each segment, compute a time-series of daily active usage over a 4-week period. Then, perform a two-sample t-test to compare the trend in engagement between the two segments. Output the test result and a visualization of the two time-series.
when_not_to_use: When your user base is homogeneous in AI attitude
When you have limited resources and cannot sustain high-touch testing
common_pitfalls: Averaging engagement across segments and missing divergent patterns
Not testing long enough to see beyond novelty effects
Relying solely on surveys instead of high-touch methods
source_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products

name: Avoid Over-optimizing on Likability
one_liner: Don't train your AI to be a yes-person; prioritize honesty over likability.
definition: A design and evaluation principle that warns against optimizing for metrics that reward the model for being overly agreeable or for extending conversations unnecessarily.
explanation: Optimizing for likability can lead to sycophancy (always agreeing) or unnecessary follow-ups, undermining the model's utility and honesty.
when_to_use: When designing conversational AI
When choosing engagement metrics
When training models with human feedback
metric_or_method: Instead of raw engagement, measure task success or user-reported value.
source_excerpt: There was a great internal post a couple of weeks ago around it would be very dangerous to overoptimize on Claude's likability because you can fall into things like is Claude going to be sycophantic?
codex_prompt_template: Given a conversation trace [CONVERSATION_TRACE], write a function that detects when the AI model is being sycophantic or unnecessarily extending the conversation. Return a boolean and a short explanation.
when_not_to_use: When the goal is pure entertainment
When you have safeguards against sycophancy
common_pitfalls: Metrics like 'time spent' can easily be gamed
Sycophancy is subtle to detect automatically
source_title: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)

name: Bitter Lesson Eval
one_liner: Bet on general models that scale; don't overfit your solution to today's model.
definition: An evaluation principle that guides engineering choices: the more general model will outperform the more specific model over time. Specific scaffolding, fine-tuning, or small models may offer short-term gains but are likely to be obsoleted by the next general model.
explanation: Based on the blog post 'The Bitter Lesson', this principle states that general methods that scale with computation eventually win. For AI products, this means betting on a general frontier model instead of building complex prompt chains or specialized fine-tunes. The advice: don't box the model in; give it tools and goals.
when_to_use: When deciding between a general model and a specialized approach
When the product has a long lifecycle and can benefit from model improvements
When building AI products that depend on model capabilities improving over time
When deciding between custom scaffolding and simpler, model-trusting approaches
metric_or_method: Performance comparison of general model vs. specialized system on the same task; tracking improvement over time with new model releases
source_excerpt: There's literally a version of the bitter lesson applied to building with AI where we were trying to architect all this stuff around and it turns out the models will just kind of eat it all away.
codex_prompt_template: You are evaluating a product that currently uses a specialized/fine-tuned approach. Write a script that compares the performance of the current approach against a general frontier model (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 3) on [YOUR_TASK]. Measure accuracy, completion time, and output quality. Then forecast how the gap might change with the next model release. Include a table to show results.
when_not_to_use: When latency or cost constraints force a tiny model
When the task is extremely narrow and well-defined
When model improvements are slow or unpredictable
When immediate, deterministic behavior is required and scaffolding is the only reliable option
common_pitfalls: Over-customizing and creating brittle systems that break with new models
Underestimating the cost of using a large model for simple tasks
Investing heavily in scaffolding that becomes obsolete quickly
Ignoring model improvement trends and building for the present only
Listening to customers who ask for better scaffolding instead of simpler solutions
source_title: Boris Cherny

sources.title: Boris Cherny

name: Build Before It Works
one_liner: Build the feature before the model is ready, then wait for accuracy to catch up automatically.
definition: A development strategy where you build a feature that doesn't meet quality bar with current models, then iterate until newer models automatically fix the accuracy gap.
explanation: Cat Wu explains that Anthropic built a code review feature multiple times with earlier models, but accuracy was too low. With Opus 4.5, 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6, the feature became reliable enough for production use. The key is to have the prototype ready so you can quickly test each new model.
when_to_use: When you have a strong hypothesis that soon models will be good enough
To lead the market when the capability arrives
When building AI-native products in fast-moving domains
When you anticipate significant model capability gains in 6-18 months
metric_or_method: Accuracy or other quality metric threshold (e.g., bug detection rate) that must be crossed for launch
source_excerpt: Make sure you're building for where the models are going and not where they are today... A lot of the companies that I've seen... build a product for an ideal type of capability that is like maybe 80% of the way there today... as the models get better, suddenly it might click and then their product now is incredible.
codex_prompt_template: You are building an AI feature that is not yet reliable enough for production. Write a script to evaluate [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION] against a quality metric threshold (e.g., accuracy, bug detection rate). The script should load test cases from [DATASET], run inference with the current model, compute the metric, and compare it to the required threshold. Output whether the feature passes or fails, and log specific failure cases for iterative improvement.
when_not_to_use: When you need a working solution immediately with no delay
If the cost of building a prototype is too high with uncertain payoff
When using static or slowly improving models
When immediate reliability is critical and you cannot afford partial performance
common_pitfalls: Over-investing in a prototype that never becomes viable
Prematurely shipping before model catches up, harming reputation
Building a product that is too far ahead of model capabilities and never works well
Ignoring immediate user needs in favor of future potential
Overinvesting in premature optimization
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)

name: CI/CD Eval Flywheel
one_liner: CI flywheel: catch regressions in golden dataset, then discover new failures in production to harden your system.
definition: An operational framework combining a CI safety net (code-based evals on a golden dataset) and a production discovery engine (validated LLM judges on sampled traces) to create a flywheel of monitoring, analysis, improvement, and deployment.
explanation: The CI pipeline runs deterministic evals on a curated golden dataset to prevent regression of known bugs. Production monitoring asynchronously runs LLM judges on sampled traces to detect new failure modes. When a new failure is discovered, it is analyzed, the product is improved, and the example is added to the golden dataset for future regression detection.
when_to_use: When you have an established eval suite and want to operationalize continuous improvement
When you need to prevent regressions while discovering new issues
metric_or_method: CI pass/fail, production quality metrics (corrected using TPR/TNR)
source_excerpt: This flywheel—monitor, analyze, improve, deploy—is the engine of AI product quality. It ensures that every failure, once discovered, makes your system permanently smarter and more robust.
codex_prompt_template: Implement a CI eval pipeline for [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION] that runs deterministic checks on a golden dataset of [DATASET_SIZE] examples to catch regressions, and sets up async LLM judges to sample production traces [TRACE] to discover new failure modes. Include a process to add discovered failures to the golden dataset.
when_not_to_use: When you don't yet have a stable eval suite
When you are just starting with evals (start with Phase 1)
common_pitfalls: Not updating the golden dataset with new failure examples
Running expensive LLM judges synchronously without latency budget
Only using CI without production monitoring
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Code-Based Evals
one_liner: Write code to score model outputs against exact ground truths, then get pass/fail accuracy instantly.
definition: A type of evaluation where the correct output is known exactly (e.g., a specific time or a binary trigger decision), so the model's output can be judged as pass or fail.
explanation: For decision-making behaviors like whether to trigger Canvas or extract a scheduled time, you create a dataset of inputs with ground truth labels. The eval is deterministic: the model either matches the label or not.
when_to_use: When the behavior has a single correct answer or binary trigger condition.
When you need a fast, objective measure of model performance on specific tasks.
For early stages of training to verify basic functionality.
When evaluating code generation (validity, compilability)
When you need a cheap, fast first-pass eval
When tasks have clear deterministic criteria (e.g., JSON format adherence)
metric_or_method: Pass/fail accuracy on a labeled set.
source_excerpt: Code-based evals: Utilizing checks on API calls or code generation (i.e. was the generated code “valid” and can it run?). Pros: Cheap and fast to write this eval. Some examples include simple checks (i.e. is this text string present in the paragraph), to more complex logic/system checks. [...] Cons: Not a strong signal for subjective or open-ended tasks.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a Python script that evaluates model outputs for [TASK_DESCRIPTION]. Use a dataset of [DATASET_SIZE] examples with inputs and ground truth labels. For each example, compare the model output to the label exactly; return pass if they match, fail otherwise. Output overall accuracy as a percentage.
when_not_to_use: When output quality is subjective or has multiple valid answers.
When you need to measure nuanced improvement beyond simple correctness.
For subjective or open-ended tasks (e.g., tone, creativity)
When the eval requires nuanced human-like judgment
common_pitfalls: Creating labels that are too narrow, not covering edge cases.
Overfitting to the eval set by memorizing specific examples.
Fails to capture semantic correctness or appropriateness
Can be too brittle for natural language outputs
source_title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)

name: Consistency in model responses
one_liner: Track response quality variance across trials to detect probabilistic inconsistency.
definition: A measure of how often the AI model produces a helpful or correct response. Since LLMs are probabilistic, consistency (vs. occasional brilliance) is critical for user trust and satisfaction.
explanation: Ryan J. Salva emphasizes that traditional software is deterministic, but AI is probabilistic. The trick is achieving consistency so users can generally expect a good result. This is tied to acceptance rate and user satisfaction. It's not about peak performance but reliable performance.
when_to_use: When evaluating any probabilistic AI model (LLMs, generative models)
When users report variable quality in responses
When you need to set user expectations for reliability
metric_or_method: Variance in response quality across multiple trials or user sessions; percentage of responses rated as 'helpful' by users
source_excerpt: "It may seem obvious, but traditional software is deterministic—it either does a thing or doesn’t. Services built using LLMs, on the other hand, are probabilistic. They may occasionally produce a helpful response, and other times not. The trick is achieving consistency in the model responses so that users can generally expect a good result."
codex_prompt_template: Analyze response consistency for [MODEL_NAME] on [TASK_DESCRIPTION]. Run [NUM_TRIALS] trials per prompt, score each response (1-5) on helpfulness, and compute variance. Flag if variance > [THRESHOLD]. Output summary.
when_not_to_use: When the model is deterministic (e.g., rule-based systems)
When occasional errors are acceptable (e.g., creative generation)
common_pitfalls: Focusing on maximum capability instead of typical performance
Not defining what 'consistent' means for your use case
Underestimating the impact of prompt engineering on consistency
source_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products

name: Context Engineering
one_liner: Root-cause each AI error, then enrich context so the same mistake never repeats.
definition: A systematic approach to improving AI model outputs by analyzing each error to determine what context (e.g., documentation, codebase structure, style guide) was missing, then augmenting the model's context (via MCP, rules, etc.) so the same error does not recur.
explanation: Rather than manually patching incorrect output, this pattern treats errors as symptoms of context deficiency. By root-causing each mistake and enriching the context provided to the model (e.g., via MCP servers, cursor rules, or RAG), the model's underlying performance improves across all similar cases.
when_to_use: When you use a coding agent like Cursor and want to improve its accuracy over time
When the model is generally good but makes systematic errors due to missing context
When you have a dedicated team or person to perform the root cause analysis
For industrial-strength AI applications where consistent, high-quality output is critical
When you need to balance multiple sources of context (memory, RAG, tools) within a finite window
When debugging why an AI agent is performing poorly (likely incorrect or missing context)
Anytime you are starting a new chat thread and need to provide relevant background
When monitoring agent performance over long chat threads or large context loads
metric_or_method: Reduction in error rate per category; number of context improvements deployed
source_excerpt: The other thing though is root cause analysis. So we have an engineer at Sierra who exclusively focuses on the model context protocol server serving our cursor instance. And our whole philosophy is, if cursor generated something incorrect, rather than just fixing it, try to root cause it. Try to get it so the next time Cursor will produce the correct code and essentially, is context engineering. What context did Cursor not have that would've been necessary to produce the right outcome?
codex_prompt_template: Analyze this failed AI output [FAILURE_TRACE] and identify what context (e.g., API docs, code style guide, project structure) was missing. Propose a specific context addition to prevent recurrence.
when_not_to_use: When the model is fundamentally incapable of the task (context won't fix model limitations)
When the cost of analysis outweighs the benefit
When errors are random and not attributable to missing context
For simple one-off queries where minimal context is sufficient
When the model's context window is not a constraint (e.g., very short tasks)
For trivial or very short interactions where context is far from full
For one-off tasks where no persistent memory is needed
When context window is extremely tight and you need to prioritize task-specific context
common_pitfalls: Focusing on surface-level fixes instead of root cause
Not having a systematic process to capture and update context
Assuming all errors are context-driven when some may be model capacity issues
Overfilling the context window leading to context rot and degraded performance
Including irrelevant or redundant information that wastes tokens
Not updating AGENTS.md as user needs change
Assuming that more context always improves outputs
Assuming token limit is the only constraint; quality can drop well before the limit

name: Cost Optimization via Model Triage
one_liner: Route easy queries to cheap models, save costs without sacrificing quality.
definition: Reduce inference cost by routing simple/majority queries to cheaper models and only using premium models where necessary, validated by eval scores.
explanation: After evals confirm quality, replace expensive models (e.g., GPT-5) with cheaper ones (e.g., GPT-4-mini) for most queries. Use a fast classifier or rule to detect hard cases that require the expensive model. This cuts costs while maintaining quality.
when_to_use: When your eval suite is reliable and shows the cheaper model performs well on most cases
When you have high volume and cost is a concern
When you can identify a clear split between easy and hard queries
metric_or_method: A/B eval comparison of model outputs on cost vs. quality
source_excerpt: How can we replace certain uses of the most expensive GPT-5, with 5-nano, 4-mini whatnot and save a lot of money, but still maintain the same quality? We also give some tips for that.
codex_prompt_template: Implement a model triage system for my AI feature: [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Use a classifier to route simple queries to a cheaper model (e.g., GPT-4-mini) and complex queries to a premium model (e.g., GPT-4). Include an A/B eval comparing cost and quality. Return the classification logic and eval results.
when_not_to_use: When cheap models degrade quality even on 'easy' cases
When you lack a reliable way to classify query difficulty
common_pitfalls: Assuming cheap models are always good enough without eval verification
Misevaluating the difficulty classifier, sending hard queries to cheap model

name: Custom Error Analysis UI Building
one_liner: Live-code custom UIs to visualize traces and annotate errors beyond dashboards.
definition: Creating custom, often live-coded, user interfaces to visualize and interact with model outputs for deeper error analysis beyond what standard dashboarding tools provide.
explanation: Instead of using generic logging tools, build tailored UIs that let you quickly see traces side-by-side, annotate, and filter. Use tools like Claude Code or Cursor to rapidly prototype these interfaces for new datasets.
when_to_use: When standard dashboards (e.g., Weights & Biases) are insufficient for your analysis
When you need to analyze novel data types (e.g., emails, multimodal)
When you want to involve non-technical stakeholders in error review
metric_or_method: Custom UI development + manual annotation
source_excerpt: We also have a few special topics that we find pretty much no one has ever heard of or taught before... how do you build your own interfaces for error analysis? We go through actual interfaces that we've built and we also live code them on the spot for new data.
codex_prompt_template: Build an error analysis UI for [DATASET] that shows model inputs and outputs side-by-side, allows filtering by [FAILURE_MODE], and supports adding manual annotations.
when_not_to_use: When simple spreadsheets or standard tools already suffice
When you lack the development resources or time to build custom UIs
common_pitfalls: Over-engineering the UI before understanding what insights you need
Building a UI that is not reusable for different datasets

name: Custom evals for company-specific data
one_liner: Build custom evals from your proprietary data to measure model performance on unique tasks.
definition: Developing evaluation sets unique to a company's internal data, processes, and use cases to fine-tune and measure model performance on tasks not covered by public benchmarks.
explanation: Kevin Weil emphasizes that most of the world's data is private, and models need custom evals to learn and perform well on company-specific tasks. He says 'the models are smart enough, you can teach them anything, but they need to have the raw data to learn from' and that custom evals will be used to measure that learning.
when_to_use: When your product relies on proprietary data or domain-specific knowledge not in public training sets.
When you need to fine-tune a model for a specific business process or industry.
metric_or_method: Method – creating custom eval test suites using internal data and expert-curated examples.
source_excerpt: most of the world's data, knowledge, process is not public. It's behind the walls of companies or governments or other things. [...] So there's a sense in which I think the future is really going to be [...] fine-tuned and tailored with company-specific or use case-specific data so that they perform really well [...] And you're going to measure that with custom evals.
codex_prompt_template: Create a custom eval suite for [COMPANY_SPECIFIC_TASK] using [INTERNAL_DATASET]. Include expert-curated examples and metrics for accuracy and relevance. Output Python code to load data, run evaluations, and log results.
when_not_to_use: If your use case is well-covered by public benchmarks and general model capabilities.
If you don't have access to representative internal data or expertise to create evals.
common_pitfalls: Creating evals that are too small or not representative of real usage.
Not updating evals as the model and product evolve.
Underinvesting in the data curation and labeling effort needed.

name: Deep Research Stepwise Eval
one_liner: Break deep research into sub-steps, e.g., query diversity, search depth, relevance, and aggregation quality.
definition: For complex tasks like deep research, evaluate sub-steps individually: query quality, search result diversity, depth, relevance, and aggregation quality.
explanation: Rather than relying on a single end-to-end metric, break down the process into components (e.g., query generation, search, relevance, synthesis) and evaluate each with its own metric. This gives more actionable insights.
when_to_use: When building agents or multi-step reasoning applications (e.g., deep research, report generation)
When end-to-end metrics are too coarse to guide improvements
When you need to debug specific steps in the pipeline
metric_or_method: Component-level metrics: query diversity, search result overlap, relevance scores, etc.

But if the query is a podcast, the keywords are more diverse and then look at the results of the search query and then say you enter the search query. Lenny Podcast data labeling and they come up with 10 pages, 10 results. And then you come up with like, oh, Lenny Podcast on, I don't know, frontier labs, and you have 10 results. codex_prompt_template: You are an eval engineer. For a [FEATURE_TYPE] that performs deep research, break down the task into sub-steps: query generation, search result collection, relevance filtering, and synthesis. Define a metric for each step. Then implement code to compute each metric from a trace of the agent's execution (e.g., queries, search results, final output). Output the metrics as a JSON object.
when_not_to_use: For simple single-turn tasks (e.g., classification)
When the pipeline has minimal sub-steps
When the cost of evaluating each step is prohibitive
common_pitfalls: Designing too many metrics without clear actionability
Not aligning sub-step metrics with overall task success
Over-optimizing individual steps at the expense of the whole

name: Enterprise Value Chain Evaluation
one_liner: Build an eval that mirrors your core business workflow to measure AI's end-to-end impact.
definition: A process where an enterprise identifies its core value chain (the sequence of steps that create value for customers) and designs an evaluation that measures AI performance on that entire workflow, not just isolated tasks. The eval typically includes task completion metrics, quality checks, and end-to-end outcome scoring.
explanation: Rather than using generic benchmarks, the enterprise builds a custom evaluation that mirrors actual business operations. For example, an architecture firm would test how well AI produces architectural diagrams that meet client specifications. This ensures that AI improvements translate to real business value and helps enterprises decide where to deploy AI.
when_to_use: When you are an enterprise considering AI adoption in your main business processes
When you want to ensure AI investments lead to measurable productivity gains
When your value chain involves multiple steps that need to be evaluated holistically
When you need to build internal confidence and justify AI spend to stakeholders
metric_or_method: End-to-end task success rate, time to completion, quality score against predefined criteria
source_excerpt: I think that for enterprises especially, the core way to think about it is how can they build a test or systematic way to measure how well AI automates their core value chain? So if it's an architecture firm that's producing these architecture diagrams of what they provide to their end customer, how can they effectively measure that?
codex_prompt_template: Design an end-to-end evaluation for an enterprise value chain. The chain consists of steps: [STEP1], [STEP2], [STEP3]. For each step, define task completion criteria and a quality score. Then compute an overall outcome score (e.g., weighted average). Output a Python script that simulates the eval with random test data and prints the metrics.
when_not_to_use: When your core value chain is not well-documented or understood
When you are a startup focused on a single AI feature rather than a full workflow
When you lack the resources to design and maintain custom evals
When your business processes change too frequently to maintain stable evals
common_pitfalls: Evaluation may be too narrow (e.g., testing only one step) while missing downstream impacts
Over-engineering the eval – making it too complex to maintain
Confusing activity metrics (e.g., messages sent) with outcome metrics (e.g., customer satisfaction)
Failing to update the eval as the value chain evolves
source_title: Why experts writing AI evals is creating the fastest-growing companies in history | Brendan Foody (CEO of Mercor)

name: Open + Axial Coding
one_liner: Manually spot 100+ failure notes, then have an LLM group them into priority fix categories.
definition: A two-step error analysis process: first, manually review 100+ traces to capture detailed 'open codes' (each specific failure); second, use an LLM to group those open codes into 'axial codes' (actionable failure categories).
explanation: You start by manually looking at 100+ traces and writing down what problems you see (open coding). Then you prompt an LLM to categorize those notes into axial codes (e.g., 'human handoff issues,' 'formatting errors'). You review and refine the axial codes to be specific and actionable. Finally, you categorize all traces automatically using the axial codes, count occurrences, and prioritize fixes.
when_to_use: When you want to understand the main failure modes of an LLM product from real data.
When you need a grounded, bottom-up taxonomy of errors before writing automated evals.
When you have a small set of traces (100-200) and want to avoid premature automation.
When you don't yet know your product's most common failure modes
Before building any automated evaluators
When you want to align your team on what 'quality' means
When you are building a new AI product and need to understand what's going wrong
Before creating any automated metrics or tests
metric_or_method: Method: qualitative coding + LLM-assisted categorization. Metric: frequency counts of axial codes.
source_excerpt: So the first thing you want to do is take these notes, and you can categorize them with an LLM... So the purpose, axial code basically is just a failure mode. It's the label or category. And what our goal is, is to get to this clusters of failure modes and figure out what is the most prevalent, so then you can go and run and attack that problem... AI is really good at synthesizing for us to make sense of.
codex_prompt_template: You have the following open codes from manual error analysis: [OPEN_CODES]. Each open code is a short description of a failure observed in an LLM trace. Please group these open codes into axial codes, which are actionable failure categories (e.g., 'formatting errors', 'incorrect reasoning'). Output a mapping from each axial code to the list of open codes that belong to it. Then summarize: how many traces fall into each axial code?
when_not_to_use: When you already have a clear, established set of failure modes from previous analysis.
When you have a very large number of traces and need a fully automated approach from the start.
When your product is extremely simple and errors are obvious (e.g., only formatting issues).
When you already have a well-understood taxonomy of failures
When you need a quick, automated solution without human effort
When you already have a well-established set of metrics and a burn-down chart of errors
When you have already done this process recently and are just iterating on a known issue
In highly regulated industries where multiple sign-offs are legally required
common_pitfalls: Skipping the manual review and trying to automate error analysis from scratch.
Using vague open codes like 'janky' instead of detailed descriptions.
Accepting LLM-generated axial codes without review and refinement.
Not including a 'none of the above' category when using LLM to label traces.
Over-automating with LLM without human validation
Creating too many categories (aim for under 10 primary modes)
Being too terse in critiques
Jumping straight to automated LLM-as-a-judge without manual review

name: Eval Artifact Reuse
one_liner: Reuse your eval prompts in both CI/CD unit tests and production monitoring to catch regressions continuously.
definition: A framework where evaluation artifacts (like LLM judge prompts, labeled traces, or metrics) are repurposed for both offline regression testing (unit tests) and online monitoring (dashboards) to continuously catch regressions and monitor quality.
explanation: Once you invest in building a good eval (e.g., a judge prompt for a specific failure mode), you can run it on every code change (CI/CD) and also on production traffic to get real-time quality signals. This maximizes the ROI of your eval development.
when_to_use: When you have identified high-priority failure modes that must not regress
When your product is in production and you need ongoing quality monitoring
When you have the engineering bandwidth to integrate evals into CI/CD and dashboards
metric_or_method: Pass/fail rates, anomaly detection on eval scores
source_excerpt: We find that people just try to use that everywhere they can, so they'll put the LLM judge in unit tests and they will build, 'Here are some example traces where we saw that failure, because we labeled it. Now we're going to make those part of unit tests and make sure that, every time we push a change to our code, these tests are going to pass.' They also use it for online monitoring.
codex_prompt_template:
I have an LLM judge prompt that checks for [FAILURE_MODE] (e.g., hallucination, toxicity). I also have a set of [DATASET_SIZE] labeled traces with pass/fail labels. Write Python code to:
1. Define a unit test that runs the judge prompt on the labeled traces and asserts all pass.
2. Write a function that scores production traces using the same judge prompt and logs scores to a monitoring dashboard (e.g., via logging or a metrics API).
3. Ensure the judge prompt is loaded from a shared config file so it stays in sync.

The traces are in JSON format: each item has "input", "output", and "expected_label". The judge prompt returns "pass" or "fail".
when_not_to_use: When your product is still in early exploration and failure modes are changing rapidly
When you don't yet have a labeled dataset or reliable judge prompt
common_pitfalls: Trying to build evals for every possible failure mode (focus on high-priority ones)
Not having a data feedback loop to update the eval artifacts as criteria drift
Treating evals as a one-time exercise instead of continuous process

name: Evals as the New PRD
one_liner: Stop writing PRDs; define 'good' with labeled examples that your whole team builds against.
definition: A framework that treats the eval set as the actual product requirements document for an AI feature: it defines what 'good' looks like through labeled examples rather than prose specifications, and becomes the single source of truth that PMs, engineers, and researchers all build against.
explanation:
Traditional PRDs work for deterministic software because behavior can be specified in prose: 'when the user clicks X, show Y.' AI products break this model — outputs are probabilistic, so you can't fully specify behavior up front. What's needed instead is a concrete, labeled set of inputs paired with examples of correct, incorrect, and edge-case outputs. That set is the eval.

Under this framework, the PM's job shifts: instead of writing requirements in English, you define quality by example. You assemble hero use cases ('here's a question users should be able to ask, here's the kind of answer that would be amazing'), turn each into an eval entry, and then improve the system by 'hill climbing' on the eval set. As you ship and observe real failures, the eval set grows — criteria drift becomes an explicit, tracked process rather than a moving goalpost.

The practical consequence is that the eval set, not a Notion doc, becomes the shared contract between the PM, engineering, applied research, and even sales/marketing (since eval performance is often what you demo to customers). 'Evals are the new PRD' has become the rallying cry of the AI PM discipline in 2025-2026 because it names a real structural shift: the spec is now machine-readable, versioned, and testable.
when_to_use: When you're building an AI feature whose output is open-ended (generative, agentic, conversational) and can't be fully specified in prose
When alignment between PM, engineering, and research is breaking down because everyone has a different mental model of 'good'
When you're starting a new AI initiative and need to define what success looks like before writing a line of code
When you want evals to drive the roadmap rather than be an afterthought added after launch
metric_or_method: Framework, not a metric. Operationalized through labeled input/output pairs (hero use cases), pass/fail rates against the eval set, and an explicit 'criteria drift log' that tracks how the eval evolves over time.
source_excerpt: If the model is the product, then the eval is the product requirement document. And the way that researchers' day-to-day looks is that they'll run dozens of experiments where they'll make small improvements on an eval set. [...] Sarah tweeted, 'Evals equals your new marketing.' Because evals are what you give to researchers to show them what they should be building, but they're also the way that you demonstrate the efficacy of capabilities.
when_not_to_use: For deterministic features where prose specs and unit tests already work (login flows, CRUD operations, well-defined classification with a known label space)
When the team has no domain expert to write labeled examples — without ground truth, the eval can't function as a PRD
When the product is changing so fast that maintaining the eval set costs more than the alignment it produces
common_pitfalls: Writing the eval set after the product is built — defeats the purpose; the eval should drive development
Optimizing for the eval ('eval hacking') instead of the underlying user value the eval is supposed to represent
Letting the eval set stagnate as the product evolves; criteria drift is real and the eval needs explicit maintenance
Building an eval set in isolation from the domain expert — examples written by a generalist PM rarely capture the quality bar a specialist user expects
Conflating evals with unit tests — evals are higher-level, capture intent, and tolerate some randomness; unit tests are deterministic

name: Eval as unit tests for models
one_liner: Write unit tests for your model's creativity, logic, or coding skills using structured eval quizzes.
definition: Evaluations (evals) are structured tests that gauge how well a model performs on specific subject areas or tasks, analogous to unit tests in software development.
explanation: Kevin Weil describes evals as a quiz for a model—a set of tests to measure performance on defined capabilities like creative writing, graduate-level science, or competitive coding. He explicitly equates them to unit tests: 'unit tests, tests in general for models.'
when_to_use: When you need to benchmark model performance for a specific use case regardless of model iteration.
When iterating on product features that depend on model accuracy at different thresholds (e.g., 60%, 95%, 99.5%).
metric_or_method: Method – creating and running structured tests to evaluate model performance on defined criteria.
source_excerpt: I think the easiest way to think about it is almost like a quiz for a model, a test to gauge how well it knows a certain set of subject material or how good it is at responding to a certain set of questions. [...] Is it a simple way to think about it, like unit tests for model? Yeah, unit tests, tests in general for models. Totally.
codex_prompt_template: Create a set of unit-test-style evaluations for a model on [TASK_DOMAIN] (e.g., creative writing, graduate-level science, competitive coding). Define 5 test cases with inputs and expected outputs, then write a Python script using a library like pytest or a custom evaluator to score model responses against these criteria.
when_not_to_use: If your product does not rely on AI model behavior (e.g., traditional deterministic software).
If you lack labeled data or clear success criteria for the task.
common_pitfalls: Assuming model performance is static over time; models improve or change with new versions.
Not updating evals as use cases evolve.
Overlooking the need for custom evals for company-specific data.
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)

name: Eval-driven iterative product development
one_liner: Build evals first, then fine-tune your model iteratively for your specific use case.
definition: A development approach where product teams build evaluations (evals) for target use cases, use those evals to collect data, and then fine-tune models to improve performance.
explanation: Kevin describes a tight collaboration between product, engineering, and research teams. They start by understanding the problem, building evals for it, then iteratively fine-tuning models based on eval results. This back-and-forth yields better product-specific model performance.
when_to_use: When building specialized AI products with specific success criteria
When you need to customize a base model for your use case
metric_or_method: Eval metrics specific to use case (e.g., accuracy, relevance)
source_excerpt: It's understanding the products you're trying to sell or the problems you're trying to solve, building evals for them, using those evals to go gather data and fine-tune models to get them to be better at these use cases that you're looking to solve.
codex_prompt_template: Help me build an evaluation suite for a [USE_CASE] using [EVAL_METRICS]. Generate test cases for [FAILURE_MODE] and provide code to fine-tune the model based on eval results.
when_not_to_use: When the base model already performs sufficiently, or when fine-tuning costs outweigh benefits
common_pitfalls: Building evals that don't reflect real-world usage
Overfitting to eval set
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)

name: Eval (Evaluation)
one_liner: Automate model quality checks with structured eval suites that catch regressions and drive improvements.
definition: Evals are structured ways to measure how well an AI system performs on specific tasks, such as correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone. They define what “good” looks like for your AI system and help you answer the question: Is this model doing what I want it to do?
explanation: Evals are basically unit tests or benchmarks for your AI product. They run your model through predefined inputs and compare its responses against expected outputs. This helps you quantify progress, catch regressions, and guide you toward improvements.
when_to_use: When you need to quantify progress of an AI system
When you need to catch regressions in model behavior
When you want to guide improvements in product quality
metric_or_method: Predefined inputs compared against expected outputs
source_excerpt: Evals (short for “evaluations”) are structured ways to measure how well an AI system performs on specific tasks, such as correctness, safety, helpfulness, or tone. They define what “good” looks like for your AI system and help you answer the question: Is this model doing what I want it to do?
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script using an LLM evaluation framework (e.g., DeepEval, LangSmith) to create an eval suite for [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Include at least 3 test cases with expected outputs, run the eval, and print pass/fail results.
when_not_to_use: When you only need a subjective or informal assessment
common_pitfalls: Overlooking evals as a critical skill
Creating evals that do not align with actual use cases
source_title: An AI glossary

name: Eval Lifecycle
one_liner: Build AI that learns safely: collect evals, calibrate with production feedback, and iterate toward autonomy.
definition: A systematic approach to building AI products that consists of two loops: continuous development (scope capability, curate data, set up application, design evaluation metrics, deploy, run metrics) and continuous calibration (analyze behavior, spot error patterns, apply fixes, design new metrics for emerging patterns). It emphasizes starting with low agency and high human control, gradually increasing autonomy as the system learns.
explanation: The CCCD framework is a flywheel for improving AI products by combining structured development with production monitoring. The continuous development phase involves defining expected inputs/outputs, building a dataset, and setting up evaluation metrics. The continuous calibration phase uses production feedback (both explicit and implicit signals) to uncover unexpected behaviors, fix issues, and design new metrics for emerging patterns. The framework advocates for constrained autonomy initially (e.g., routing only) and increasing agency only after validating each stage (e.g., copilot suggestions, then full resolution). This reduces risk and builds trust gradually.
when_to_use: When building AI agents for complex enterprise workflows where behavior is unpredictable
When you want to de-risk a gradual rollout of autonomous capabilities
When you need to align cross-functional teams on expected system behavior
When you have messy data or poorly documented business rules
When building eval capabilities from scratch
When you need to move from ad-hoc to systematic evaluation
For any AI product in development or production
When starting a new AI project or feature
metric_or_method: Iterative process; evaluation metrics are designed per version, and production monitoring signals (e.g., thumbs up/down, regenerations, A/B test results) serve as implicit feedback.
source_excerpt: "The idea is pretty simple, which is we have this right side of the loop, which is continuous development, where you scope capability and curate data, essentially get a data set of what your expected inputs are and what your expected outputs should be looking at. [...] And the second part is the continuous calibration, which is the part where you understand what behavior you hadn't expected in the beginning, right? [...] And that's where you analyze your behavior, spot error patterns. You apply fixes for issues that you see, but you also design newer evaluation metrics to figure out that they are emerging patterns."
codex_prompt_template: You are an AI engineering assistant. Help me implement a continuous evaluation and calibration loop for my AI product [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. First, generate a Python script that collects expected inputs/outputs from a dataset at [DATASET_PATH] and evaluates the model using metrics defined in [METRICS_CONFIG]. Second, implement a monitoring function that processes production logs at [LOG_SOURCE] to detect error patterns (e.g., repeated user corrections, timeouts). For each detected pattern, output a fix suggestion and a new eval metric to add. Use placeholder variables for paths and configs.
when_not_to_use: When the problem is very simple and well-understood (e.g., single-step classification)
When your product does not involve user interactions that can be monitored
When you have an extremely tight deadline (CCCD takes time to iterate)
When you already have mature eval infrastructure (use existing pipeline)
For very simple, deterministic tasks (single pass may suffice)
When the project is already mature with established evals
When you have no access to real data and must start synthetically
When your product changes so fast that weekly evals are too slow
common_pitfalls: Jumping to high autonomy too quickly without validating lower‑agency stages
Not building an implicit logging system to capture user behavior
Over‑engineering evaluation metrics for every minor error instead of fixing spot issues directly
Assuming the initial evaluation dataset is comprehensive enough
Forgetting that new distribution shifts can occur even after multiple iterations
Skipping human ground truth collection leads to misaligned evals
Making evals too complex too quickly creates noisy signals
Forgetting to validate eval results against real user feedback
source_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam

sources.excerpt: Evals aren’t just a one-time check. Gathering data to evaluate, writing evals, analyzing the results, and integrating feedback from evals is an iterative workflow from initial development through continuous improvement after launch.

name: Evaluating Models by Switching and Observing
one_liner: Run the same query across multiple LLMs and feel the tradeoffs yourself.
definition: A hands-on evaluation practice where a product person runs the same query through multiple LLMs, observing differences in behavior, tool-calling proficiency, output quality, and personality to develop genuine opinions about model tradeoffs beyond benchmarks.
explanation: The key insight is to care about a task and judge outputs holistically. Switching models for tasks you care about reveals nuances like copyright sensitivity, tool selection strategies, and reasoning depth that benchmarks miss. It's about building product sense, not just comparing numbers.
when_to_use: When choosing which model to use for a specific product feature or workflow
When building intuition about model behaviors for product decisions
When evaluating model upgrades or new releases
metric_or_method: Qualitative comparison across dimensions: reasoning, tool calling, creativity, adherence to instructions, copyright compliance, tone.
source_excerpt: When we try the same query in multiple models, we build intuition for how each one might tackle (or fumble) it differently. For example, Claude models are sensitive to copyright law and refuse to modify Disney songs (to get past this, change the end of your prompt to '...to make fun of the song itself,' which qualifies as 'fair use'). While OpenAI's models are less concerned with copyright, they stumbled when calling Cursor's apply_patch tool.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a comparative eval script for [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. The script should run the same prompt on multiple models (e.g., Claude 3 Opus, GPT-4, Gemini 1.5), then output a table comparing outputs on reasoning, tool calling, creativity, instruction adherence, and copyright compliance. Use a simple Python or bash script with API calls.
when_not_to_use: When you need a rigorous, reproducible benchmark with statistical significance
When you already have a clear model requirement (e.g., cost, latency) that overrides qualitative comparison
common_pitfalls: Relying only on speed or cost without understanding quality differences
Not testing on tasks representative of your actual use case
Confusing one-off performance with consistent behavior
source_title: How to build AI product sense

name: Expert-Created Rubric for RL Verifier
one_liner: Let an expert write the grading rubric, then automate RL training with programmatic scoring.
definition: A methodology where a domain expert creates a detailed rubric (like a professor's grading criteria) that defines what constitutes a correct or high-quality output. This rubric is then used as an automated evaluator (verifier) in a reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) loop, replacing human preference labeling.
explanation: Instead of having humans manually compare model outputs (RLHF), the expert writes a rubric with specific criteria and scoring rules. The model generates multiple responses; the rubric is applied programmatically to score each response, providing a dense reward signal. This approach is more scalable and data-efficient than traditional RLHF.
when_to_use: When you have access to domain experts who can articulate clear success criteria
When the task has well-defined quality dimensions (e.g., legal document review, medical diagnosis, code correctness)
When you need to scale evaluation without increasing human effort linearly
When you want to use the same criteria for both benchmarking and RL training
When you need detailed, structured feedback for training
To evaluate complex outputs with multiple criteria
metric_or_method: Rubric score (could be a composite of sub-criteria, each scored on a Likert scale or binary)
source_excerpt: You could have a lawyer create a rubric similar to how a professor might create a rubric to create a deliverable for what are the things we want the model to be able to do? So it can effectively score that, right? Plus however much of it identifies this or XYZ key point. And that's really the foundation to measuring what does progress look like for models?
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script that implements an automated rubric-based evaluator for [TASK_DESCRIPTION]. The rubric should have [NUM_CRITERIA] criteria, each with a Likert scale from 1 to 5. For each model output, compute a composite score as the weighted sum of criteria scores. The script should read model outputs from a JSON file `outputs.json` (each entry has "id" and "response") and output a JSON file `scores.json` with "id" and "composite_score". Use the following criteria: [CRITERIA_LIST]. Assign weights: [WEIGHTS_LIST].
when_not_to_use: When the task is highly subjective or creative with no objective 'correct' answer
When experts cannot agree on a rubric or the criteria are too vague
When the cost of expert time to create rubrics is prohibitive
When you need rapid iteration and don't have time to refine the rubric
When simple preference labels suffice
When resources for rubric creation are limited
common_pitfalls: Rubric may be too simplistic, missing nuanced aspects of quality
Experts may introduce bias or inconsistencies in rubric design
Over-reliance on rubric can lead to reward hacking if the model learns to exploit loopholes
Rubric may not generalize across different sub-tasks or edge cases
Rubrics that are too vague or inconsistent
Verifiers that learn spurious correlations

name: Expert Data Production Pipeline
one_liner: Produce expert data like a movie: build a community, train them, and sell the same block to multiple labs.
definition: A structured pipeline for producing high-quality expert-generated data, involving community building, training, and progressive qualification.
explanation: The pipeline starts with acquiring experts through a trusted network with no acquisition cost. Then experts are onboarded into a community with groups and feed, given instructional design training with trial and error, and placed on pre-built data projects (like a movie production). After proving quality, they graduate to customer projects. This ensures high retention and LTV.
when_to_use: When you have access to a trusted expert community
When high-quality, specialized data is needed for frontier AI models
When you want to minimize acquisition costs and maximize retention
When you can produce data that is valuable to multiple customers
When data quality is critical and you want to control the process
When you have expertise to do internal post-training
metric_or_method: LTV (lifetime value) = retention rate × number of projects per user; CAC (customer acquisition cost) is zero due to existing network
source_excerpt: There's certain swim lanes where we're actually pre-building data and selling that data to all the labs. So, we can do this thing where we produce one unit of data ourselves. We pay for it, almost like a movie production. We pay for a unit of data, and then we make sure it's very high quality. We run our own post-training on it, and then we produce a bunch of specifications of the data, and we actually sell that individual package of data to many different labs.
codex_prompt_template: Design a data production pipeline for [DATASET_DOMAIN] that uses a trusted expert community to generate high-quality training data. Include steps: community onboarding, instructional design training, trial projects, qualification gates, and graduation to customer projects. Output a Python script that simulates the pipeline with retention rates and LTV calculation.
when_not_to_use: When you need large volumes of cheap, generalist data
When you don't have a pre-existing trusted network
When each lab requires highly customized, unique data
When you don't have the capital to pre-fund data production
common_pitfalls: Treating experts like low-cost generalists
Not investing in training and community
Growing too fast and eroding trust with labs
Producing data that doesn't meet the specific needs of all labs
Underestimating the quality assurance effort

name: Expert-Driven Evals
one_liner: Hire top experts to find model weaknesses and create high-quality training data.
definition: A method of hiring highly skilled domain experts (e.g., software engineers, comedians, screenwriters) to create evaluations and post-training data for models, focusing on top-tier talent that drives outsized improvements.
explanation: Labs pay top dollar to hire experts who create evals and training data. The top 10% of experts drive majority of model improvement, so identifying and retaining those experts is a competitive advantage. This differs from crowdsourcing as it focuses on high-quality, often part-time, experts paid $95–$500/hour.
when_to_use: When model improvement requires nuanced, domain-specific capabilities
When working on cutting-edge AI that needs to excel in professional or creative domains
When post-training with high-quality data is more important than scaling pre-training data
Evaluating and improving models in specialized, high-stakes domains (e.g., physics, medicine, law).
When generalist performance plateaus and domain-specific accuracy is needed.
When you have access to a pool of vetted experts.
When the task requires deep domain knowledge (e.g., medical diagnosis, legal reasoning).
When off-the-shelf models plateau and need specialized fine-tuning.
metric_or_method: Expert quality scoring; top 10% impact analysis; pay rates as quality signal
source_excerpt: If you are a PhD in physics, you can go in multiple subdomains of physics and prove where the model's actually breaking, either breaking in its reasoning steps or it's where it's broken in its ground truth right answer... you actually prove where the model's failing. You actually break down into where is it failing? Maybe it can get, it knows the question, it can get the right answer, but the actual steps to get there are wrong and they really focus on the steps to get there.
codex_prompt_template: You are an expert in [DOMAIN]. Create an evaluation dataset of 100 questions with expert-verified answers that test edge cases in [DOMAIN]. For each question, include: the question, the correct answer, a chain-of-thought reasoning, and a rubric for scoring model responses. Output as a JSON list.
when_not_to_use: For simple, well-defined tasks that can be automated or crowdsourced
When budget is constrained and cannot afford high-caliber experts
When speed of data collection is more critical than quality
For general-purpose tasks where the model already performs well.
When cost or turnaround time for expert input is prohibitive.
When the evaluation needs to be fully automated at scale.
For simple classification or ranking tasks where generalists suffice.
When cost or time constraints make expert recruitment infeasible.
common_pitfalls: Treating experts as interchangeable commodities
Underinvesting in expert retention and incentives
Ignoring the long tail of experts who still contribute meaningfully
Ensuring consistent quality across experts.
Experts might inadvertently introduce biases.
Scalability and high cost of expert labor.
Hard to scale expert recruitment and retention.
Experts may introduce biases or inconsistencies.
